Classical Arabic prosody (ʿilm al-ʿarūḍ) was systematised by al-Khalīl ibn Aḥmad al-Farāhīdī (d. c. 786 CE), who identified sixteen base metres (buḥūr, sing. baḥr). The most prestigious is the ṭawīl (long), structured as: fāʿilun mafāʿīlun fāʿilun mafāʿilun per hemistich. The kāmil (complete) and wāfir (abundant) metres rely exclusively on mutatafaʿilun and mufāʿalatun feet respectively. ʿArūḍ analysis (taqṭīʿ) requires marking long (—) and short (∪) syllables and matching them to a canonical pattern (mīzān).
